title: Sea-level rise from land subsidence in major coastal cities
methods: kriging-style spatial interpolation with variance quadtree

data_scope.spatial_coverage: 48 largest coastal cities worldwide (population >= 5 million in 2020, within 50 km of coast, with elevations within 5 m MSL)
data_scope.time_period: October 2014 to April 2020 (~6 years per city)
data_scope.rlls_range_across_cities: -16.2 to 1.1 mm/yr (median); -16.2 to 1.1 mm/yr vs. IPCC AR6 VLM -5.2 to 4.9 mm/yr
data_scope.cities_with_peak_subsidence_faster_than_: 6 (Tianjin, Ho Chi Minh City, Chittagong, Yangon, Jakarta, Ahmedabad)

core_conclusion: Standardized Sentinel-1 InSAR processing across the 48 largest coastal cities (20% of global urban population, 2014-2020) reveals local land subsidence rates reaching -16.2 mm/yr, far exceeding IPCC AR6 VLM estimates (-5.2 to 4.9 mm/yr). Fast-subsiding cities are concentrated in Asian megacities. The method enables consistent global comparison of relative sea-level rise vulnerability, showing that local land subsidence is a major, underestimated driver of RSLR that empowers local mitigation action.
why_it_matters: First globally consistent, standardized InSAR-derived RLLS dataset for 48 major cities; reveals that IPCC AR6 systematically underestimates VLM variability; demonstrates within-city heterogeneity requiring local-scale intervention; enables locally actionable mitigation (groundwater management) independent of global climate negotiations.
why_nature: Planetary-scale standardized analysis; corrects a major IPCC policy-relevant blind spot; links geophysics to urban sustainable development (SDG 11); fully open data and code (DR-NTU repository).
added_value_to_my_research: Overlay RLLS maps with WorldPop and deprivation layers -> population-weighted subsidence-exposure equity score using R2SFCA-style spatial aggregation
Extend InSAR pipeline to road/infrastructure graphs -> physics-informed GNN predicting infrastructure failure probability from RLLS + structural features
Apply spatiotemporal GP decomposition to InSAR time series -> non-linear RLLS acceleration maps analogous to my spatiotemporal KDE methods
Couple heterogeneous RLLS fields with hydrodynamic flood models -> flood-reduced healthcare accessibility surface for coastal megacities
Train MGWR + SHAP explainable ML on 48-city RLLS distributions ~ urban density/groundwater/geology features -> spatially varying drivers of coastal subsidence

related.title: Land subsidence risk to infrastructure in US metropolises
related.relation: directly extends the subsidence-mapping agenda to inland US cities and infrastructure damage quantification

related.title: Social disparities in neighborhood flood exposure in 44,698 urban neighborhoods in Latin America
related.relation: adds equity and socioeconomic dimensions to the paper's RSLR hazard maps

related.title: Progress and gaps in climate change adaptation in coastal cities across the globe
related.relation: systematic assessment of coastal adaptation readiness — the policy context this paper's RLLS data feeds into

related.title: Stress-testing the cascading economic impacts of urban flooding across 306 Chinese cities
related.relation: couples flood hazard with economic impacts — natural extension of this paper's inundation modelling examples

related.title: Global geo-hazard risk assessment of long-span bridges enhanced with InSAR availability
related.relation: applies InSAR to infrastructure risk assessment — shares the same remote sensing methodology and subsidence-monitoring framing

related.title: Disproportionate flood exposure for slum populations of the Global South
related.relation: quantifies differential flood vulnerability for informal settlements — highly relevant to the Asian megacities identified as fast-subsiding in this paper

figures.caption: Peak RLLS velocities (95th percentile) across all 48 coastal cities — global map and histograms showing Asia-concentrated fast subsidence
16th/50th/84th percentile RLLS vs. IPCC AR6 VLM and GIA across 48 cities — RLLS range far exceeds global VLM benchmarks
InSAR-derived RLLS velocities and standard deviations for Jakarta (fast subsidence) — localized clusters in Penjaringan and Kembangan District
InSAR-derived RLLS velocities for New York (low subsidence) with GNSS comparison — localized subsidence at Breezy Point only
